word_list.pronunciation: dɹˈæɡ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ θɹuː ðə mˈʌd mˈaɪɚ

senses.definition: to make unfavorable remarks about someone

examples.x: I can't believe he's been dragging my name through the mud for years without my knowledge.
She drags his name through the mud by spreading rumors about him.
They dragged his name through the mire by accusing him of a crime he didn't commit.

senses.definition: the action of publishing or spreading unpleasant details about someone's private life in order to negatively impact the public's opinion of them

examples.x: By the time the scandal broke out, the media had already found enough dirt on him to ensure his political downfall.
I don't have any dirt on him, but I'll keep my eyes and ears open.
She dug up some dirt on him and used it to ruin his chances of getting the job.

senses.definition: one's personal matters that if shared with others could make one embarrassed or ashamed

examples.x: If you don't want to air your dirty laundry in front of your friends and family, you should keep your personal problems to yourself.
She's always airing her dirty laundry in public, talking about her personal problems with anyone who will listen.
The company's financial troubles have been aired in the media, and now everyone knows their dirty linen.

senses.definition: something that receives a lot of attention for a short time, but is soon forgotten

examples.x: If the company doesn't innovate and stay relevant, it will become a nine day wonder.
She is worried that her success will be a nine day wonder and that she won't be able to sustain it.
The latest celebrity scandal will be a nine day wonder and will soon be forgotten by the public.
The new gadget was a nine day wonder, but its popularity quickly faded once people realized its limitations.

senses.definition: to lose favor, respect, or a position of high regard due to a significant mistake, scandal, or failure

examples.x: The once-beloved actor's scandalous behavior caused him to fall from grace in the eyes of the public.
After years of success, the company's unethical practices led to a dramatic fall from grace in the business world.
The politician's corruption scandal resulted in a swift and public fall from grace, tarnishing their previously pristine reputation.
The sports star's doping revelations caused him to fall from grace, with fans and sponsors distancing themselves from him.
The respected academic's plagiarism scandal led to a significant fall from grace within the academic community, damaging their credibility.

word_list.pronunciation: lˈeɪ ɐ ɡlˈʌv ˌɑːn ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to cause a loss of respect or reputation for a person or thing

examples.x: The critic failed to lay a glove on the acclaimed movie, which received overwhelmingly positive reviews.
The journalist tried to lay a glove on the politician during the interview, but the politician skillfully deflected the tough questions.
The opposing team couldn't lay a glove on the star player, who scored all three goals in the match.

senses.definition: to do something that causes others to have less respect for one

examples.x: The company lost face when their highly publicized product launch failed to meet expectations.
The politician lost face after being caught in a scandal, causing their once-loyal supporters to turn against them.

senses.definition: the state of losing all the respect others had for one because of one's actions

examples.x: The company experienced a significant loss of face after their product failed to meet quality standards and caused customer complaints.
The politician suffered a loss of face when their controversial policies were met with widespread public backlash.

senses.definition: used when one says or does something that makes one disgraced or discredited
used to refer to something is not currently appropriate, popular, or available due to being outside of its usual time or season

examples.x: The restaurant was serving pumpkin spice lattes in May, which were out of season and not on the menu.
The store was selling winter coats in the middle of summer, which were out of season and not in demand.

senses.definition: to be subjected to disapproval, rejection, or criticism from others, often through booing

examples.x: The comedian's offensive jokes caused them to get the bird from the audience, who began booing and hissing loudly.
The politician was surprised to get the bird from the crowd during their speech, as they had expected a more positive reception.

senses.definition: to make a mistake that brings about a negative consequence

examples.x: He blotted his copybook by getting caught cheating on the exam.
He is blotting his copybook by constantly missing his deadlines.
I fear I have already blotted my copybook by failing my last exam.
If you don't improve your behavior, you will blot your copybook and damage your chances for promotion.
She always arrives on time, but she blots her copybook by being rude to her colleagues.

senses.definition: to find the flaws and weaknesses of something, like a plan, action, or idea

examples.x: The editor picked holes in the manuscript, pointing out every mistake and inconsistency.
The teacher picked holes in the student's essay, criticizing every minor error and omission.

word_list.pronunciation: mˌeɪk ɐn ˈæs ˈaʊt ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to make someone appear foolish or ridiculous in front of others

examples.x: He's constantly bragging about his accomplishments, and it's making an ass out of him.
She always interrupts others in meetings, and it makes an ass out of her.

word_list.pronunciation: pˌʊt hæv wˈʌnz hˈɛd nˈɛk ɑːnðə blˈɑːk

senses.definition: to take a particular course of action that endangers one's reputation or position

examples.x: He's putting his neck on the block by quitting his stable job to start his own business.
I have my head on the block by betting all my savings on a risky stock.
If he decides to confront the boss about his unfair treatment, he'll be having his neck on the block.
She puts her head on the block every time she invests in a new startup.
They have put their heads on the block by taking out a large loan to fund their dream project.

senses.definition: to behave in an embarrassing or foolish manner in front of other people, in a way that that makes them mock one

examples.x: He is making an exhibition of himself by arguing loudly with his friend in the restaurant.
If you keep shouting and making a scene, you'll make an exhibition of yourself and embarrass us all.
She always makes an exhibition of herself by singing loudly in the office.

senses.definition: a negative perception of someone or something

examples.x: The company's poor handling of the environmental incident gave them a black eye in the public's eyes.
His involvement in the scandal left his political career with a significant black eye.
The team's loss in the championship game was a black eye for their otherwise successful season.
The company's unethical business practices have given the entire industry a black eye.
The controversial remarks made by the celebrity actor resulted in a black eye for their public image.
